There is one single fundamental substance which is God. God is his might, God is his goodness, God is his will. So the difference between classical theology and your idea of seeking a single propositional primitive is that the ground is an essence, which is the basis for all truth.
https://sunriseoath.substack.com/p/where-have-you-arrived

By the proper perfect meaning of "Jesus is beauty", it is equally true that "Jesus is a master" and "Jesus is a creator" and "Jesus is the writer of your story", etc. It just is not a competition. They mean the same thing. The reason why I talk about the victory and sovereignty aspect is to drive home aseity more than anything else.

The thing is that something cannot be perfectly beautiful except insofar that it is infinitely creative, and something cannot be perfectly good except insofar that it is infinitely powerful. So each one of these terms is necessary *and* sufficient. The human connotation of each merely analogizes to God and some analogies are better than others, but in God there is only one perfect essence.

Divine simplicity seems like a weird place to start, maybe, but it also leads to quite beautiful results. And notably, it can challenge *any* attempt to order different possible aspects we highlight about God. If someone wanted to elevate winning above love, they also just get smacked with about the same tirade about how they are one and the same in God. 

I say to you again and again that God is victory because it honestly just seems like the most interesting thing to say, haha. At the very least, it leads to the most conversation. It is also a very Calvinist thing to say, of course; but I think it is a very Calvinist thing to say precisely because it is a polemic against the completely to be expected reductive interpretation of "God is love".
https://x.com/sunriseoath/status/2072007904585044033?s=20

In classical theology we never have to deal with something like "winning in an ugly way", because when we think that this is possible we are defining either victory or beauty wrong. A great way to order our ontology.

It would be a tragedy if we never moved on from Mozart. He was a unique genius given his time and situation, but he certainly was not more of a genius than, say, Palestrina. The reason why we talk so much about Mozart is because he represents something that we cannot do anymore (improvisation with classical voice leading), which feels close yet is too far from our current understanding. I hope that one day Mozart will be a little more obscure and we might have a new Moby Dick to hunt down.

Religion is nothing more than a certain mode of language, and I do not think that before heaven and earth are one we will ever not have language. The English language *is* a religion, anyway; people get just as dogmatic about English as they do about anything else.

Godly language, with godly religion as a subset, will always be important precisely because godly language is language which actually has good consequences. There are better and worse ways of speaking; so long as we bother to say anything well at all and there is any payoff to "communication" (communion), there will be religion. This is even as any religion we see today might not be visibly present in the far future, by our own ontology.

Sacrifice should in my view be seen less as a kind of exploratory technique in its own right and rather the result of the perfect strategy. The times where a perfect poker strategy loses money count as a kind of sacrifice or scourging; and even when one "rises from the dead" and wins back the losses, the scars are still on one's body.

It is useful to apply the Ongian categories of orality vs. literacy. I posit that there is a broad similarity between "indigenous ways of knowing", the hippies of the counterculture, rednecks, communists, white nationalists, and Third Worldists.

TPOT (and ORI, which is probably best seen as a movement emerging from TPOT) is interesting because by historical standards it is highly literate and technological, but comes out of an off-the-cuff riffing that is more characteristic of oral culture. We are the children of digital secondary orality.

Written thinking also allows for "importing" long modules much more easily. The thing about oral wisdom is that it is all packaged for oral transmission, meaning something being pithy with a clever rhyme is highly valued. Written wisdom is packaged for written transmission. (Video is a weird case of a kind of "text" that also has oral properties. Which is what makes it so powerful and even dangerous when used for mass influence.)

Also, an "oral search engine" is almost just muscle memory of where one might say a word and what comes next, and so is necessarily just tagged based on vibes and symbolic relevance. Being able to exhaustively search a fixed index is what promotes thinking that survives rational scrutiny. (Ong's thesis is that media completely shapes our cognition and priorities.)

Written logic can also be "retraced" by just taking the same document and editing it. To retrace oral logic, things have to be repeated or recalled, which makes disputation quite costly. A mark of oral culture is dismissing lengthy adversarial conversation and going for short cleverisms.

I don't think that a normie actually wants to seem like an autist; they want to seem what an autist seems like in their domain of autism, which is a skilled and fluent practitioner of a symbolic system. The common failure mode is of course when a symbolic system diverges from its referent or purpose. (More Baudrillard). Arguably autists are the most susceptible to the drift between a symbology and its referent, even when they are updating their use of the denotative qualities. (The normie tends to care about the surface level connotation a lot, which actually has a lot of wisdom. Ignoring connotation or thinking one is above considering it results in bad cognitive security.)

"Autists" are driven by other motivations while consciously operating a symbology for the purpose of 1 (yet perhaps holding it too tightly). The best autists "chase after God", so to speak, always letting their fluent symbology be sanctified toward truth. The worst autists are just idolators.

Also, though it is true that the sociopath operates on layer 4 and is what someone like George (from TPOT) would call a "frame wizard" (he said this about me to someone before, LOL), I think that the best autists use these layers as a symbolic system in themselves and can operate it toward Truth. First was when your work first came up, second was yesterday when i followed a link to the PLOS ONE paper on AHAH, third is today, because this is really important stuff. And, man,  the lightning example. You coin two terms, anti-hebbian and hebbian. Anti-hebbian is searching in a diffusion-like way - like this picture of lightning before it finds its preferred path from the video you include

For comparison, this image shows that and "Hebbian" behavior which is when the lightning locks on. I wish the direction were switched and the flows started at the top, like lightning, but this image should be the first thing people see for an explainer, and be the hook.

I'm narrating how I understand this as I read it for the first time because you clearly want to be understood and for people to see your work - and I'd wager that - because hebbian/anti-hebbian as a concept is such a foundational part of your toolkit (a load-bearing premise, which ORI's A/B/U system would assign to category B) that seeing it from our perspective - where our mental models are still in an anti-hebbian mode - takes more energy.

And this concept puts a name to something important in teaching - it's why a math teacher can be a fields medalist and make no sense to his son in third grade. All the possible explanations or ways which math could work - like the sky below the source of the lightning - are open to the student, while the teacher can only easily 'see' the areas directly adjacent to his locked-on, Hebbian perspective. Or maybe in the interest of time, I'll just share: from working with dimensional reduction tools and maps, I've found that "betweens" and "centers" is a really concise way to share who we are. Like "(1) what are the identities do you feel between, and have a unique blended perspective on? and perhaps more difficult to answer (2) what are the identities you are more at the center of, and perhaps unable to see as well?"

This was the hack I found for really situating myself, and it's often easy to be quite vulnerable. Our betweens and centers say a whole lot about who we are in culture, in very few words.

I usually put slides about my own at the front of presentations, and have heard positive feedback. It's easy to keep updating them as your life moves, and it's a nice introspective inventory

I also find it might prevent people from accidentally choosing "a culture", as sometimes our culture is defined as our boundary position between cultures. Doing this sort of dynamic inventory of identities and cultures might help people be their most complex selves, which I suspect is the site of the work that ppl (at least the sort of ppl you're talking to) are called to do 🙂

Centers can be really abstract, and mind-expanding: situating self in the cosmos, or the ecology, or the family, or the conscious mind. So it can model a sort of multi-scalar thinking that I think would serve your exercise 💜
"inventory of centers and betweens" is what I call it. It's a good icebreaker when ppl are meeting one another too, and prompts lots of launching-off points for conversations between ppl 😄

"O Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner."

This prayer is well loved in both the West and the East of Christianity, despite different conceptions of sin. For the Eastern Orthodox (who analogize sin more to a sickness), it means practically: O healer, please put me, a sick person still untreated, on the triage list. For Rome and the Protestants alike (who analogize sin more to a legal condition and disposition), it means more: O judge, please deal with me, a guilty person and repeat offender, with fairness and compassion. 

I think that a key thing I would add to what you said is that rather than there being positive or negative emotions in themselves, there is a landscape of different experiences that are better or worse (by their actual outcomes, which could include diagnostic features) and which are marked by certain primary emotions. Good and evil, or better and worse, is more of an ordering of possibility (both what we model and what we desire to see) than it is about qualia. If we use the language of classical theology, to say that something is evil is akin to saying that it should not come to be or will not come to me. (It is in this sense that evil is of the will and not of actuality.)

There is of course also an initial innate value hierarchy of emotions, almost like a pecking order in the command room of Inside Out. But growing up requires reordering this hierarchy, over and over. "Love conquers all" is really powerful in this context, since it basically says that investing in the actuality of any instance one is in and ordering one to see the wisdom in it and desire in it and still consider thriving in it (Jesus quoting Psalm 22 on the cross is an act of poetic defiance against those who scourged him).

In 1 Corinthians 13, the order of emotion is faith < hope < love. To translate, this is reliance, expectation, and coincidence / abidance.

message: using the mind as a lab

what do they mean by the self? 

when I read tweets or philosophical claims about the self I often just don't understand what entity they are talking about

I don't have an intuition of what they mean (alternative I suppress my intuition and previous analysis in order to think freely)

to answer the question i would just contemplate it in various ways. some intellectual, and some meta-cognitive monitoring, basically just using any exploratory tool I have to look for an internal experience or representation that I can point to and say "yeah when they talk about the self they mean *this* internal thing I'm focusing on right now"

and I couldn't find an internal experience that is relevant or relatable to the concept of self as I understand it being used by others

my contemplation process doesn't happen in one dedicated block of focused time, I muse about it and have some intermediary day dreams and distractions

in some idle moments while day dreaming I happened to be reminiscing about some interaction I had with a colleague and my "sense of self" became a recognizable and tangible phenomenon within my experience, so the goal directed circuits always in the background waiting to flag new info that might be relevant for my main activity (contemplating the self) triggered my meta-cognition and I thought "wait a minute, this experience of having a sense of self seems relevant"

so I used my mind as a lab and used memories and imagination to reminisce and construct scenarios all with the goal of playing with this sense of self that I am now tracking

so it occurred to me that the sense of self is connected to social emotions such as pride, envy, guilt, shame etc. a scenario where I undergo e.g. social defeat or victory gives me a sense of self

this idea of self is unlikely to entirely wrong, it probably contains some truth but let's say it turned out to be false

the value in making false theories is that I would be using this theory as a rival candidate for any of the other theories I create or come across, they would have to be better explanations than this one

and this provides an initial guide and additional layer of criticism for any rival theory and also a scaffolding or ladder for understanding our best theory

the right theory should be able to contextualize this wrong theory and say exactly which parts are wrong and why

this would ultimately be nothing but an aid for our understanding and perhaps it is even indispensable as understanding itself is a counterfactual phenomenon, you don't understand why something is the way it is without understanding how it could have been otherwise and why it isn't that way. if you don't have this referencing and contextualizing false ideas then you have simply memorized a theory and I'd wager you don't really understand it at all

Also agree you don't understand Daoism given the questions being asked. Particularly, the divesting from all values bit. Daoists are obsessed with values. The whole De part of the Dao De Jing is about values. Ideally, you hold values lightly so you can swap them as needed. I think everyone does this to some degree, but Daoists view this as a skill to find the best set of values for any context.

Identity isn't monatomic and it's made out of a bunch of values sets that people swap around. Often this swapping is gradual to the point people don't notice they're doing it and even pointing it out can trigger people since it's breaking their context and can look contradictory.

I think about Daoism like a philosophy that allows you to be aware of this tool switch and not get stuck with a hammer in your hand when you need a screwdriver. Big is saying that he can't tell you how he's deciding to swap tools but that it happens on its own while he recognizes it.

I don't think he's divesting reason and emotion for action. More that these are merged into action to be indistinguishable. Daoists will likely trip up somewhat on "truth" since this is an alien concept and there's no close lexical equivalent in any of classical Chinese and ontology comes before evaluation of true/false. Calling something "real","true", or "actual" doesn't make sense while being understandable. Encountering this words you have to peek more into their evaluation frames rather than accept its ontological significance.

Despite there being some sense in which knowledge can be ranked, no super granular and distilled knowledge ranking has really stood the test of time; and "lower knowledge" cannot really be purely "upgraded" to "higher knowledge", like how the existence of university does not abolish kindergarten. Theology being queen of the sciences is an old idea, and generally Christians would say the Trinity is more important than an exact ordering of angels, but at some point some arithmetic probably helps more for navigating creation (though not spirituality) than even fairly foundational theology.

Looking at Elo systems might illustrate what I mean. It is clear that there are better or worse players given the fact that different people experience different outcomes in the game, and certain game concepts like tempo and positional advantage are foundational to most understanding resulting in the best outcomes; but it is not that clear that we could easily do something like a stratified Elo where we target single chess skills, at least not without them just becoming false metrics when taken too seriously.

Tactics puzzles are  a concrete example, where being able to solve very difficult puzzles (themselves ranked by Elo) is worth *something*, but some players are just way better or worse at tactics than their Elo suggests.

Just because brain cells are not themselves minds and often distract from minds does not mean that we should seek to have less brain cells to unveil the mystery of the minds.

There is never flat remembrance because there is always the need to recall, even in reading. Every word read is in practice a mnemonic for the rest of the life of the reader.

There always has to be an operator, no matter how sophisticated the operation. The primary reason for disillusionment from complex operations is that one feels the operator is taken out of the picture, but this is false.

Having a desire to store *less* information and then citing a dislike for the "flat" remembrance of things matches pretty well to this mind of disillusionment. It really reads to me as, "I want the focus to be on what people consider true and salient, so I will make it harder to find the things that are not true and salient". Essentially trying to force the issue and require immediate creation of memory devices for a whole literature instead of just providing the literature.

The logical conclusion of this is indeed either a paradigm where lay Catholics were barred from reading the Bible (and instead had to rely on a mix of orality and basic literacy) or even a return to oral culture where things that are not relevant are forgotten in two generations. But this to me is anti-technological and even godless.

When I was trying to get The Syllabus off the ground, the idea was that some things which are always going to be there in the literature will just sometimes be forgotten books on a shelf. If someone ever saw a link to it in the future, they could still read it. But it might not be relevant for a long time; just that when it is, it is available. 

The only downside of this is if the storage of the book costs a lot of resources. With plaintext, this is not really an issue. Especially not with Discord which is practically made for storing community chat logs.

You are much better off just encouraging people to index the whole literature according to salience and then encouraging them to get high signal by looking to those indexes, rather than trying to do some clever ergodic tricks. Ergodicity could be interesting as an optional feature, but in the end it relies (like surprise parties) on ignorance and lack of imagination.

I point again to the parable of the wheat and the tares. The time for the tares to be separated from the wheat is upon the harvest, at the point of judgment. This happens when people read a document or look through a server or perform a search query. This does not happen at the level of a whole literature itself.

A published book is an interesting example where the point of judgment is before the actual text is assembled, where the totality of all writing is shaped into the "final" version. A published book is necessarily not an actively growing document. What makes ORI interesting is that it is actively growing. Someone could distill down ORI writings into books, but those books would not be ORI itself.

I also think that deciding to destroy or hide writings as a form of selection or to set into stone a judgment about what should and should not be read is only good if one lacks a vision of how others could benefit from the process notes in the future. There are many things in my Obsidian vault which were pretty much useless when I wrote them, but ended up being useful later. If I acted as the judge early, I myself would be under judgment.
It really does start and end with this: a platform should encourage more thriving, not encourage an aesthetic concern apart from thriving. (Aesthetics must let its followers thrive to be good.)

the pitch I gave some guy asking what I'm working on:

I grew up Christian and became atheist rationalist (specifically critrat) and then went into effective altruism and also I discovered tpot mystics 

so I have essentially 4 different cultures inside of me and they conflict with one another

so I set out initially to create a bridge between the rationalists and the mystics/religious through philosophical means, essentially trying to work on the cultural repos rather than my own personal repos

and I think Tom Hyde's very recent and incomplete stance called wonderism provides the first true philosophical bridge that achieves what I wanted to achieve. he tries to reconcile the Romantic movement and Enlightenment Rationalism which are currently considered adversaries 

so I adopted the identity of a "romantic rationalist" because I am trying to extend Tom Hyde's wonderism way further than he would himself take it

the core idea is simple, axiology (the study of value, what is good, what is important) precedes things like ethics and aesthetics (and forms an unexplored part of epistemology)

.

the core problem today is the two camps have different value schemas (a concept I created)

the first schema is:

emotion > logic

this represents the religious, mystics and romantics (you can replace emotion with intuition or romance or whatever, and logic with reason, science, rigor or whatever)

one is more important than the other, in art, in ethics, in epistemology etc

the second schema is:

logic > emotion

this represents the rationalists

so that is the core conflict, it's not really a conflict of theories or ideas it's a conflict of values, if you value emotion or intuition enough then you can "take stories (or feelings etc) seriously" in the same way you can take ideas or theories seriously, so it resolves a lot of the disputes

romantic rationalism has this schema:

emotions == logic

they are equally important, like electricity and magnitism, they unify to make the electromagnetic force

I currently believe value schemas are a unifying concept in various fields of philosophy including aesthetics, ethics, memetics, comparative culture and comparative art/literature, phenomenology

I think of it like interpretability research, where cultures and religions and works of art etc are black boxes that you can open using the tool of the value schema, you can analyze utterances, and rather than comparing the concepts (which is impossible because they use different sign systems entirely) you compare the value schemas encoded in their speech

e.g. a Daoist says the Dao that can be named is not the true Dao, and when you speak to one they will tell you that you value words too much

from that it's clear there is a value structure there and it's clear that this value structure conflicts with e.g. Christianity's emphasis on the Word and definitely with the rationalists (commonly mockingly called wrodcels)

finding the value structure allows you to compare it to other religions and make sense

.
